description.en: Combine DataFrames using merge (SQL-style joins), join (index-based), and concat (stacking rows or columns).

story_variants.en:
## Combining DataFrames

Real data rarely lives in one table. Pandas provides three main ways to combine DataFrames.

### pd.concat — Stacking

Stack DataFrames vertically (more rows) or horizontally (more columns):

```python
import pandas as pd

df1 = pd.DataFrame({'id': [1,2], 'name': ['Alice','Bob']})
df2 = pd.DataFrame({'id': [3,4], 'name': ['Charlie','Diana']})

stacked = pd.concat([df1, df2], ignore_index=True)
# id     name
# 0  1    Alice
# 1  2      Bob
# 2  3  Charlie
# 3  4    Diana
```

### pd.merge — SQL-style Joins

```python
orders = pd.DataFrame({'order_id': [1,2,3], 'cust_id': [10,11,10], 'amount': [100,200,150]})
customers = pd.DataFrame({'cust_id': [10,11,12], 'name': ['Alice','Bob','Charlie']})

result = pd.merge(orders, customers, on='cust_id', how='left')
```

### Join Types

| how= | Keeps |
|------|-------|
| `'inner'` | Only matching rows in both DataFrames |
| `'left'` | All rows from left, matching from right |
| `'right'` | All rows from right, matching from left |
| `'outer'` | All rows from both, NaN where no match |

### df.join — Index-based Joining

```python
df_a = pd.DataFrame({'score': [90,85]}, index=['Alice','Bob'])
df_b = pd.DataFrame({'age': [25,30]}, index=['Alice','Bob'])

joined = df_a.join(df_b)
```

### Merging on Multiple Keys

```python
pd.merge(df1, df2, on=['year', 'month'])
```

> **Key Insight:** `pd.merge` is the most powerful and flexible — it mirrors SQL JOIN syntax. Use `concat` when you simply want to pile DataFrames on top of each other, and `join` when working with index-aligned data.

explanation.en: Vertically stack two DataFrames; reset index with ignore_index=True
Left join: keep all orders, attach customer info where available
Index-based join: align on row labels

right.label: LEFT join
right.note: All from left, NaN where no match

instruction.en: Create two DataFrames: `employees` (emp_id, name) and `salaries` (emp_id, salary). Merge them on `emp_id` using an inner join and store the result in `merged`.

practice_challenges.test_code:
import pandas as pd
assert isinstance(employees, pd.DataFrame) and 'emp_id' in employees.columns
assert isinstance(salaries, pd.DataFrame) and 'salary' in salaries.columns
assert isinstance(merged, pd.DataFrame)
assert 'name' in merged.columns and 'salary' in merged.columns, 'merged must have both name and salary'

instruction.en: Create three DataFrames `q1`, `q2`, `q3` each with columns `product` and `units_sold` for a different quarter. Stack them into one DataFrame called `annual` using `pd.concat`.

practice_challenges.test_code:
import pandas as pd
assert isinstance(annual, pd.DataFrame)
assert len(annual) == len(q1) + len(q2) + len(q3), 'annual must contain all rows from all quarters'
